_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — bank pytań P2 „Bezpieczne używanie AI”, moduł MB5 (AI w pracy: polityki i shadow AI)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo, Jan Syntetyczny, identyfikatory testowe). ZERO realnych danych/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu MB5 (Q141-Q146), pula dedykowana ścieżki P2. Bez pytań krytycznych. Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant understands the concept of shadow AI and distinguishes it from approved tool use.
questions.prompt: What does the term “shadow AI” mean in a company?

options.text: An official pilot program for new AI tools run by the IT team.
Dark mode in AI assistant apps.
An AI model run locally on company servers instead of in the cloud.
Employees using AI tools without the company’s knowledge and consent, with no control over the data that ends up there.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Shadow AI is unapproved, uncontrolled use of AI tools at work. The problem is not the tool itself but the lack of control: nobody knows what company data is flowing out and under what terms it is processed.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Shadow AI is using AI tools without the company’s knowledge and consent (D) — an analogy to “shadow IT”. An IT pilot, dark mode, or local hosting is not shadow AI. Review the definition from the module.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant knows who is responsible for content produced with the help of AI.
questions.prompt: With the help of AI, you are preparing an offer for a SklepDemo customer. The offer includes a product parameter made up by the model. Who is responsible for this error?

options.text: Nobody — errors generated by AI cannot be attributed to anyone.
You — AI is a tool, and the person who signs off on the content they send is responsible for it.
The AI model vendor — it was their product that generated the error.
The customer — they should have verified the parameters themselves before signing.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: AI is a tool like a text editor. The content you sign and send is yours — along with the duty to verify it. This is the foundation of responsible AI use at work.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The person who signs off on content sent to a customer is responsible for it (B). AI vendors’ terms of service explicitly exclude their liability for generated content, and shifting verification onto the customer risks losing trust and contractual consequences.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant chooses policy-compliant behavior when the approved tool is not enough.
questions.prompt: Company policy allows only the internal AI assistant to access customer data. You discover a new public tool that would do your analysis of customer tickets twice as fast. What do you do?

options.text: I use the new tool, but only for smaller tasks — at a small scale the risk is negligible.
I use the new tool from my private laptop — the company policy does not cover private equipment.
I report the tool to IT/the security team, proposing an assessment and possible approval; until then I work with the tool allowed by the policy.
I anonymize the data “by eye” and put it into the new tool — after all, I removed the names.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: the approval path exists precisely so that new tools are assessed in a controlled way instead of being used in secret. By reporting the tool, you combine innovation with security.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A small scale, a private laptop, or amateur anonymization does not lift the policy — customer data still flows out of the company’s control. Correct: report the tool for assessment and work with the approved one until it is cleared (C).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant takes copyright and licenses into account when using AI-generated content in company materials.
questions.prompt: You are creating a public brochure for NotkaApp. An image generator produced the perfect graphic, but it resembles the recognizable style of a well-known brand, and you do not know the generator’s license terms. What do you do?

options.text: I publish it — AI content is not subject to any rights, so there is nothing to check.
I check the generator’s license terms (whether it allows commercial use) and consult about the graphic resembling someone else’s style before publishing anything.
I publish it with a caption “generated by AI” — labeling the source takes care of the legal issues.
I slightly modify the graphic with a filter — after the changes, copyright no longer applies.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: commercial use requires checking the tool’s license, and similarity to someone else’s brand is a legal risk regardless of who (or what) produced the graphic. You consult on doubts before publishing.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: AI content is not “free of law”: the generator’s license defines permitted use, and similarity to someone else’s brand risks infringement regardless of a caption or a filter. Correct: check the license and consult (B).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies what a company AI usage policy typically defines.
questions.prompt: What does a company AI usage policy typically define? Select all that apply.

options.text: The list of AI tools approved for work use.
Which categories of data may be processed in which tools.
Employees’ private purchases in online stores.
The favorite AI models of individual board members.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An AI policy answers two questions: WHAT you may work with (approved tools) and WITH WHAT (the data categories allowed for each tool). It is a map of safe use, not a list of bans for their own sake.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The core of an AI policy is approved tools (A) and permitted data categories (B). Private purchases and someone’s preferences are not the subject of an AI usage policy.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant analyzes the consequences of shadow AI in a team and chooses a corrective response instead of escalating the cover-up.
questions.prompt: Scenario: you discover that half of your team has for months been pasting descriptions of customer tickets into private accounts in a public AI chat, because “everyone does it and it’s faster”. The results are good, the customers are happy. Which assessment of the situation is correct?

options.text: Since the results are good and nobody has reported a problem, the practice can be considered proven and left alone.
It is enough to ask the team for discretion — the risk only appears when the practice comes to light.

This is systemic shadow AI: customer data has been flowing out of the company’s control for months, regardless of the quality of the results. It must be disclosed, pasting customer data must stop, and a request for an approved tool must be raised.
The only problem is using private accounts — it is enough to set up a shared team account in the same public chat.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Accurate analysis: good results do not compensate for the uncontrolled outflow of customer data — the risk (legal, contractual, reputational) grows with every paste. The fix = disclose, stop, give the team a legitimate alternative.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The quality of the results does not remove the risk: customer data flows out of the company’s control regardless of outcomes and discretion, and a shared account in the same tool changes nothing. The correct assessment: C — disclose, stop, provide an approved alternative.
